fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this graph computation service that our data pipeline team keeps asking about. Basically it reads a JSON blob from stdin and spits out plain text results — should be pretty self-contained. The tricky part is making sure the outputs are always deterministic and reproducible regardless of how the internals work, since different engineers might implement this differently and we need consistent results across environments.

There are a bunch of operations we need to support — the usual stuff like looking up neighbors, checking if edges exist, doing traversals, finding shortest paths (both weighted and unweighted), spanning trees, that kind of thing. Also need the cycle detection and the component partitioning thing we talked about in the architecture review.

One thing that burned us last time with the login module compatibility logic — make sure errors come back as clean neutral category strings, not whatever the runtime throws. Nobody downstream wants to parse Java stack traces or Python exceptions.

Also the ordering thing is critical — wherever results could come out in random order, we need them sorted canonically so tests don't flake. I know we had issues with this on the old graph service.

Oh and there should be a test runner script under rcb_tests/ that can point at a directory of JSON cases and tell us pass/fail. The cases themselves have an input and expected_output field. Target is all 24 ops working correctly.

trigger_keywords: how should edges be displayed in output
edge rendering format undirected vs directed
what notation for edges in text output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Undirected edges must be rendered as `a--b` with `a <= b` (smaller id first). Directed edges must be rendered as `a->b`. This applies consistently across all operations that output edge lists, including traversal results, remove operations, spanning tree/forest edges, and DOT export.

trigger_keywords: what error categories are allowed
how should errors be reported
error message format for invalid inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Errors must be reported as neutral category strings on a line beginning with `error=`. The defined categories are exactly: `vertex_not_found`, `edge_not_found`, `vertices_not_found`, `negative_edge_weight`, `negative_cycle`, `requires_directed`, `requires_undirected`. No runtime exception names, stack traces, or language-specific message text should appear. Each error line is followed by relevant context lines (e.g., `vertex=9` after `error=vertex_not_found`).

trigger_keywords: how are vertex ids assigned
what is the vertex id numbering scheme
how do I reference vertices in edge definitions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Vertex ids are assigned by array position: the vertex at position `k` in the `vertices` array gets integer id `k`, so ids run `0..N-1`. All edge endpoints, query fields (`vertex`, `from`, `to`, `start`, `end`, `source`, `target`), and output references use these positional ids, not the stored vertex values.

trigger_keywords: how should unspecified ordering be handled in output
what sorting rules apply to output sequences
canonical output ordering for sets of vertices or edges
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any sequence whose order is not determined by the algorithm must be canonicalized before output. Vertex id sequences and component member lists are emitted in ascending numeric order. Edge sets are sorted canonically (normalize each edge so the smaller endpoint is first for undirected, then sort lexicographically). Comma-separated lists on a single line use ascending order. This ensures the same input always produces byte-identical output.

trigger_keywords: what does the test runner script need to do
how should test.sh work
test runner output file naming convention
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test runner must be at `rcb_tests/test.sh`. It accepts `--cases-dir <subdir>` (defaulting to `test_cases`). For each JSON case file it runs the program with the case's `input` field piped to stdin, writes raw stdout to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<stem>@<NNN>.txt` (where NNN is a zero-padded case index), compares against `expected_output`, and at the end reports a pass/total tally.

trigger_keywords: how does degree reporting work for undirected graphs
undirected graph indegree outdegree definition
degree semantics for non-directed edges
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For undirected graphs, each incident edge counts equally toward out-degree, in-degree, AND total degree. Concretely: outdegree and indegree both equal the number of incident edges, while total degree also equals that same count (not double). See the spec example: vertex 0 with one incident edge reports `outdegree=1 indegree=1 degree=1`.

trigger_keywords: what format does the DOT export need
how should vertex labels appear in dot output
dot serialization format details
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: DOT export starts with `graph` (undirected) or `digraph` (directed) on its own line. Then sorted vertex lines: `<id> [label="<value>"];`. Then sorted edge lines: `<a> -- <b> [label="<weight>"];` for undirected or `<a> -> <b> [label="<weight>"];` for directed. The vertex value (not the id) appears as the label. Edges are sorted the same way as elsewhere.

trigger_keywords: what happens when spanning tree root cannot reach all vertices
spanning tree from disconnected graph
spanning_tree_from when graph is not fully connected
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For `spanning_tree_from`, if the graph is not fully connected from the given `start` vertex (i.e., not all vertices are reachable), output exactly `spanning=none` as a single line. Only report the tree (edge_count, total_weight, edge list) when `start` can reach every vertex in the graph.

trigger_keywords: how should negative weight edges be handled in Dijkstra
negative edge weight error reporting format
which ops reject negative weights
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Operations `shortest_path_weighted` and `heuristic_search` must check for any negative-weight edge in the graph before running. If found, output `error=negative_edge_weight` followed by `from=<id>`, `to=<id>`, `weight=<value>` for the first offending edge encountered. The `negative_aware_paths` operation uses Bellman-Ford and instead reports `error=negative_cycle` (with no context lines) if a negative cycle reachable from source is detected.

trigger_keywords: what is the input format for each test case file
JSON case file structure
how are test cases organized in the file
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each feature test file (e.g., `featureN_*.json`) is a JSON object with a `description` string and a `cases` array. Each element of `cases` has an `input` object (the JSON to pipe to stdin) and an `expected_output` string (with literal `\n` newlines). The test runner iterates over all cases in the array, numbering them `000`, `001`, etc. for output file naming.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'summary', 'neighbors', 'edge_existence', 'vertex_value', 'edge_weight', 'remove_vertex', 'remove_edge', 'add_edge_invalid', 'degrees', 'traverse_bfs', 'traverse_dfs', 'detect_cycle', 'topological_order', 'strongly_connected_components', 'shortest_path_unweighted', 'shortest_path_weighted', 'single_source_paths', 'negative_aware_paths', 'heuristic_search', 'all_pairs_shortest_paths', 'spanning_forest', 'spanning_tree_from', 'coloring', 'export_dot'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error normalization rules defined in the PRD's 'Error normalization' section: using stable neutral category strings like `vertex_not_found`, `edge_not_found`, etc. instead of runtime exceptions — the same philosophy of hiding internal implementation details from consumers.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the ordering thing is critical — wherever results could come out in random order
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'Determinism constraint' and output format rules in the PRD: all unordered sets (vertex sets, edge sets, component members) must be sorted ascending before output; comma-separated id lists use ascending numeric order; undirected edges are normalized to `a--b` with `a <= b`.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the component partitioning thing we talked about in the architecture review
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 14: `op=strongly_connected_components` — Kosaraju's or Tarjan's SCC algorithm for directed graphs, outputting `count=N` then one line per component with sorted member ids, components emitted in ascending order of their smallest member.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a JSON blob from stdin and spits out plain text results
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The program reads exactly one JSON object from stdin (with fields `op`, `directed`, `vertices`, `edges`, plus op-specific fields) and writes newline-terminated ASCII text to stdout, one item per line, as specified in the 'Common input fields' and 'Output format' sections of the PRD.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect off the top of my head — can you check the start.md spec file in the repo root? It should have the precise contract for that operation. If it's still ambiguous after reading it, flag it and we can loop in the architect.

feature01_graph_summary.json.description: Report the basic size of a constructed graph: whether it is directed, and its vertex and edge counts.

feature02_neighbor_lookup.json.description: List the adjacent vertices of a given vertex (outgoing side for directed graphs), in ascending order.

feature03_edge_existence.json.description: Answer adjacency queries: report whether a directed edge from one vertex to another is present or absent.

feature04_vertex_value.json.description: Look up the stored value of a vertex by id; a lookup for a non-existent vertex is reported as a not-found error.

feature05_edge_weight.json.description: Look up the weight of the edge between two vertices; a missing edge is reported as a not-found error.

feature06_remove_vertex.json.description: Remove a vertex and all its incident edges, then report the resulting graph contents.

feature07_remove_edge.json.description: Remove a single edge between two vertices, then report the resulting graph contents.

feature08_add_edge_invalid.json.description: Attempt to add an edge; if either endpoint does not exist it is rejected as a not-found error, otherwise it is accepted.

feature09_degrees.json.description: Report out-degree, in-degree and total degree for every vertex, in ascending vertex order.

cases.expected_output:
vertex=0 outdegree=2 indegree=0 degree=2
vertex=1 outdegree=1 indegree=1 degree=2
vertex=2 outdegree=0 indegree=2 degree=2

vertex=0 outdegree=1 indegree=1 degree=1
vertex=1 outdegree=2 indegree=2 degree=2
vertex=2 outdegree=1 indegree=1 degree=1

feature10_traversal_bfs.json.description: Breadth-first traversal from a start vertex; report the set of edges followed to reach newly discovered vertices.

feature11_traversal_dfs.json.description: Depth-first traversal from a start vertex; report the set of edges followed to reach newly discovered vertices.

feature12_cycle_detection.json.description: Determine whether the graph contains a cycle.

feature13_topological_order.json.description: Produce a topological ordering of a directed graph; report whether the graph is acyclic and, if so, the ordering.

feature14_strongly_connected_components..description: Partition a directed graph into strongly connected components; report the count and each component's sorted members.

feature15_shortest_path_unweighted.json.description: Find a shortest path between two vertices counting hops (edges), ignoring weights; report the path and hop count, or none.

feature16_shortest_path_weighted.json.description: Find a minimum-weight path between two vertices; report path and total weight, none if unreachable, or reject a negative edge weight.

feature17_single_source_paths.json.description: Compute minimum-weight paths from one source to every reachable vertex; report each target's weight and path.

cases.expected_output:
target=0 weight=0 path=0
target=1 weight=4 path=0,1
target=2 weight=7 path=0,1,2

feature18_negative_aware_paths.json.description: Compute single-source minimum-weight paths allowing negative edge weights; report each target, or reject a negative cycle.

cases.expected_output:
target=0 weight=0 path=0
target=1 weight=4 path=0,1
target=2 weight=2 path=0,1,2

feature19_heuristic_search.json.description: Find a minimum-weight path between two vertices using a guided search; report path and total weight, or reject a negative edge weight.

feature20_all_pairs_shortest_paths.json.description: Compute the matrix of minimum path weights between every ordered pair of vertices; unreachable pairs are reported as inf.

feature21_spanning_forest.json.description: Compute a minimum spanning forest of an undirected weighted graph; report edge count, total weight and the chosen edges.

feature22_spanning_tree_from.json.description: Grow a minimum spanning tree from a start vertex of an undirected weighted graph; report the tree, or none if unreachable.

feature23_coloring.json.description: Assign colors to vertices so adjacent vertices differ; report vertex count, whether the coloring is proper, and the number of colors used.

feature24_export_dot.json.description: Serialize the graph to a DOT description; report the graph kind and the sorted vertex and edge lines.
